questions.text: Which one of the following waves require a material medium for their propagation:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: X-ray waves are electromagnetic waves. They do not need a medium for their propagation as they can travel through a vacuum.
Light waves are also electromagnetic waves. Thus, there is no need of medium for propagation
y-rays are electromagnetic waves as well. So they don't need a medium for propagation
Sound Waves are mechanical waves. Thus they will need a medium for propagation

questions.explanation: Electromagnetic Waves do not need a medium for their propagation; they can travel in a vacuum. For Example: Light Waves, Radio waves, Gamma rays, etc.Mechanical Waves like Sound Waves do need a medium for their propagation. For example: Earthquakes and water waves.

questions.text: Which one of the following scientists regarded light as electromagnetic waves:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: James Clerk Maxwell was one of the greatest scientists of the nineteenth century. He is best known for the formulation of the theory of electromagnetism and in making the connection between light and electromagnetic waves.

Louis de-Broglie was a French physicist, who is known for his remarkable proposal regarding the nature of nature. He reasoned how light is dualistic, behaving like a wave in some situations and like a particle in some.

Electromagnetic waves as described by Maxwell's field equations do not satisfy Newton's Laws as described in Principle. That is one of the major discrepancies that led to relativity theory. EM waves are not mechanical waves so they are not well described by Newtonian physics

Galileo did not make the relationship between light and electromagnetic waves and is more known for his telescopic observations on the mountains of the moon, moons of Jupiter etc, and his study regarding gravity and free fall.

questions.explanation: Maxwell was a Scottish physicist who was regarded as being the scientist to regard light as electromagnetic waves.

questions.explanation: "All motions are relative."This statement reflects the principle of relativity in physics, which is a fundamental concept introduced by Albert Einstein in his special theory of relativity. The concept of motion is relative, meaning that the motion of an object can only be described in relation to another object. There is no absolute state of rest or absolute state of motion; it all depends on the observer's frame of reference.

questions.text: An ideal black body is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: A body whose absorber power is unity
options.explanation: Refer to the main explanation box.
Refer to the main explanation box.

questions.explanation: A black body absorbs all incident radiation without reflecting or transmitting any of it. It emits radiation across a broad spectrum of frequencies based solely on its temperature. This emission follows Planck's law. The absorptivity of a black body is the same for all wavelengths and angles of incidence. The emitted radiation spectrum is continuous and depends on the temperature of the black body. Warmer black bodies emit more radiation, and the peak of the emission shifts to shorter wavelengths with increasing temperature. The intensity (brightness) of radiation emitted by a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature, as described by the Stefan-Boltzmann law. These characteristics are theoretical ideals, and while no real object perfectly meets these criteria, certain objects, like stars or specially designed cavities, can approximate the behavior of a black body under specific conditions.

questions.text: Pair production occurs only when energy of photon is at least equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that pair production occurs when the energy of the photon is at least 1.02 kiloelectronvolts (keV). However, this is not correct. The energy of the photon required for pair production is much higher than this.

This option proposes that pair production occurs when the energy of the photon is at least 1.02 megaelectronvolts (MeV). This is the correct answer. Pair production is a process in which a photon with energy greater than or equal to 1.02 MeV interacts with the electric field of a nucleus (or an electron) and converts its energy into an electron-positron pair.

This option suggests that pair production occurs when the energy of the photon is at least 1.02 electronvolts (eV). However, this is incorrect. The energy requirement for pair production is much higher than the energy of typical photons in the eV range.

This option proposes that pair production occurs when the energy of the photon is at least 1.02 gigaelectronvolts (GeV). However, this energy requirement is much higher than what is needed for pair production. GeV-scale photons are associated with high-energy processes in particle physics but are not relevant to pair production.

questions.explanation: Pair production occurs when the energy of the photon is at least 1.02 MeV. This process is an essential phenomenon in particle physics, where a high-energy photon creates an electron-positron pair in the presence of a nucleus or an electron.

questions.explanation: Photons are massless particles. The rest mass of a photon is zero. Unlike particles with mass, such as electrons or protons, photons do not have rest mass.
